passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Animals & Biology
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: Animal Communication — How Animals Talk to Each Other
Passage 2: Animal Migration — The Greatest Journeys on Earth
Passage 3: Ecosystems — How Nature Works Together
Passage 4: Endangered Animals — Species at Risk of Disappearing
Passage 5: Insects — The World's Most Important Animals
Passage 6: Social Animals — Life in Groups
Passage 7: Oceans — The Blue Heart of Our Planet
Passage 8: Animal Intelligence — How Smart Are Animals?
Passage 9: Forests — The Lungs of the Earth
Passage 10: Animal Adaptations — Surviving in Extreme Environments

Passage 1: Animal Communication — How Animals Talk to Each Other
Topic: Animal Behaviour  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Communication is essential for survival in the animal world. Animals use a wide variety of methods to send messages to each other — to find a mate, warn others of danger, defend their territory, or coordinate as a group. Unlike humans, most animals cannot use spoken language to communicate. Instead, they rely on sounds, body language, scent, colour, and movement to get their messages across. Some of these communication systems are surprisingly sophisticated. Scientists who study animal behaviour have discovered that some species can communicate in ways that are much more complex than people once thought, and that understanding how animals communicate can teach us a great deal about the evolution of language itself.
Paragraph B
Sound is one of the most common forms of animal communication. Birdsong is a well-known example — male birds sing to attract females and to warn other males to stay away from their territory. Whales and dolphins use complex patterns of sound — sometimes called "songs" — to communicate across very large distances in the ocean. Bats produce high-pitched sounds that bounce off objects, helping them navigate and find food in the dark — a process called echolocation. Elephants communicate using very low sounds that humans cannot hear, called infrasound, which can travel through the ground for several kilometres. Even insects communicate through sound — male crickets rub their legs together to produce chirping sounds that attract females.
Paragraph C
Animals also communicate through body language and visual signals. Many species use bright colours to send messages. Male peacocks spread their enormous, colourful tail feathers to attract females. Poison dart frogs display brilliant colours — red, yellow, and blue — to warn predators that they are toxic and dangerous to eat. Honeybees perform a remarkable "waggle dance" to communicate the direction and distance of a food source to other bees in the hive. Chimpanzees and other primates use a wide range of facial expressions, gestures, and body postures to communicate emotions and intentions — similar in some ways to how humans use non-verbal communication.
Paragraph D
Scent is another very important communication channel for many animals. Dogs and wolves mark their territory by leaving their scent — through urine and glands in their paws — so that other animals know the area is claimed. Many animals release chemicals called "pheromones" to communicate with members of their own species. Female moths release pheromones that can attract male moths from several kilometres away. Ants use pheromones to lay a chemical trail that other ants follow to find food. Interestingly, research has shown that even some human behaviour may be influenced by chemical signals that we are not consciously aware of. The study of animal communication continues to produce fascinating discoveries about the many ways in which living creatures connect with each other.

Passage 2: Animal Migration — The Greatest Journeys on Earth
Topic: Animal Migration  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Migration is one of the most remarkable behaviours in the animal kingdom. Every year, millions of animals — birds, fish, mammals, insects, and even sea turtles — make long journeys between different habitats, often travelling thousands of kilometres. Animals migrate for several main reasons. Many migrate to follow the seasons, moving to warmer areas in winter and returning when the weather improves. Others migrate to find food — following the movement of prey or the growth of plants. Many animals also travel to specific breeding grounds — places where they return each year to find a mate and raise their young. Migration requires enormous energy and can be very dangerous, yet it has evolved in hundreds of species because the benefits outweigh the costs.
Paragraph B
Some of the world's most impressive migrations are made by birds. The Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any animal on Earth — it travels from its breeding grounds in the Arctic all the way to Antarctica and back every year, a round trip of about 70,000 kilometres. This means an Arctic tern may travel a distance equivalent to three trips to the Moon and back over its lifetime. Bar-tailed godwits — a type of wading bird — fly non-stop from Alaska to New Zealand, a journey of about 11,000 kilometres, without landing or eating for up to nine days. Swallows, geese, and millions of other bird species also make impressive seasonal migrations between their summer breeding grounds and warmer winter habitats.
Paragraph C
Migration is not limited to birds. Every year, millions of wildebeest in East Africa make a circular journey of around 3,000 kilometres across the Serengeti Plains and into Kenya, following the seasonal rains that bring fresh grass to feed on. This is known as the Great Migration and is considered one of the most spectacular wildlife events in the world. In the ocean, humpback whales travel from their summer feeding grounds in polar waters to warm tropical seas to breed in winter. Salmon are famous for their remarkable return migration — they hatch in rivers, swim out to sea, and then return to the exact same river — sometimes after several years in the ocean — to spawn and then die.
Paragraph D
How animals navigate during migration is one of the most fascinating questions in biology. Many birds use the position of the sun and stars to find their direction. Some animals, including certain species of birds and sea turtles, can detect the Earth's magnetic field and use it like a compass. Salmon use their sense of smell to find their way back to the exact river where they were born. Some species simply follow experienced older animals who have made the journey before. Climate change is now threatening migration patterns around the world — changes in temperature and weather are shifting the timing of seasons, reducing food availability along migration routes, and causing habitat loss at both ends of these remarkable journeys.

Passage 3: Ecosystems — How Nature Works Together
Topic: Ecosystems & Biology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
An ecosystem is a community of living things — plants, animals, fungi, and tiny microorganisms — together with the non-living parts of their environment, such as soil, water, air, and sunlight. All the parts of an ecosystem are connected and depend on each other. Energy flows through an ecosystem in the form of food. Plants capture energy from the sun through a process called photosynthesis and use it to grow. Animals that eat plants — called herbivores — get their energy from the plants. Animals that eat other animals — called carnivores — get their energy from the animals they hunt. When living things die, bacteria and fungi break down their bodies and return nutrients to the soil, which plants then use to grow. This flow of energy and nutrients is called a food chain or food web.
Paragraph B
Ecosystems exist in a delicate balance. Each species plays a role that affects many other species. When one species is removed or its numbers change dramatically, it can have a significant impact on the whole ecosystem — like removing a thread from a woven cloth, which can cause other threads to loosen and unravel. Wolves are a famous example. When wolves were hunted to extinction in Yellowstone National Park in the United States in the early 20th century, the deer population grew enormously because there were no predators to control it. The deer ate so many plants that vegetation along rivers was destroyed, which caused the riverbanks to erode. When wolves were reintroduced in 1995, the ecosystem began to recover — even the rivers changed their course as vegetation grew back and stabilised the banks.
Paragraph C
The world contains many different types of ecosystems, each with its own unique collection of species and conditions. Tropical rainforests are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth — they cover only about 6% of the land surface but are home to over half of all known plant and animal species. The ocean is the largest ecosystem on Earth, covering more than 70% of the planet's surface and supporting an enormous variety of life — from the tiniest plankton to the largest whales. Coral reefs are sometimes called the "rainforests of the sea" because of their incredible biodiversity. They cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support around 25% of all marine species. Grasslands, deserts, wetlands, and polar regions each provide a unique habitat for the species that have adapted to live there.
Paragraph D
Human activity has damaged many of the world's ecosystems. Deforestation removes the habitat of countless species. Pollution contaminates soil, rivers, and oceans. The introduction of non-native species — animals or plants brought to a new area by humans — can disrupt ecosystems by outcompeting native species. Climate change is altering temperature, rainfall, and sea levels in ways that many species cannot adapt to quickly enough. Scientists estimate that we are currently losing species at a rate up to 1,000 times faster than the natural background rate — a mass extinction event driven almost entirely by human activity. Protecting and restoring ecosystems is not just important for wildlife — healthy ecosystems provide essential services for humans too, including clean air, clean water, fertile soil, and protection from flooding.

Passage 4: Endangered Animals — Species at Risk of Disappearing
Topic: Extinction & Conservation  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
An endangered species is a type of plant or animal that is at risk of becoming extinct — meaning that every individual of that species dies, and it disappears from the Earth forever. Extinction is a natural process — species have always come and gone throughout Earth's history. However, the current rate of extinction is many times higher than it would naturally be, and scientists believe that human activity is the main cause. Today, thousands of species are considered endangered, including many well-known animals such as tigers, elephants, rhinoceroses, orangutans, and polar bears. Once a species becomes extinct, it is gone forever — and its loss can have knock-on effects on the entire ecosystem it was part of.
Paragraph B
There are several main reasons why species become endangered. The most common is habitat destruction — the removal or damage of the places where animals live. Deforestation, for example, destroys the forest homes of countless species. Urban development, farming, and the draining of wetlands all reduce the amount of natural habitat available to wildlife. Hunting and poaching — the illegal killing of animals — is another major cause. Rhinos are hunted for their horns, which are used in some traditional medicines; elephants are killed for their ivory tusks. Tigers and leopards are killed for their skins. Pollution also harms wildlife — chemicals from farming, factories, and waste disposal contaminate the water and soil that animals depend on. Climate change is an increasingly serious threat, altering the habitats and food supplies of many species.
Paragraph C
To protect endangered species, conservationists use a range of strategies. The most basic is creating protected areas — national parks and nature reserves — where wild animals can live without being hunted or having their habitat destroyed. Anti-poaching units patrol these areas and try to prevent illegal hunting. Captive breeding programmes keep endangered animals in zoos and wildlife centres, allowing them to reproduce in a safe environment. If the numbers in the wild become very low, animals bred in captivity can be released back into protected wild areas. International agreements, such as the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES), help control the buying and selling of endangered animals and products made from them across national borders.
Paragraph D
Some conservation efforts have had impressive results. The giant panda — perhaps the world's most famous endangered animal — has seen its status improve from "endangered" to "vulnerable" thanks to decades of conservation work in China, including habitat protection and a successful captive breeding programme. Tiger numbers in India have grown significantly after the Indian government introduced strict protection laws and increased patrols in tiger reserves. The mountain gorilla, once on the brink of extinction with fewer than 700 individuals, has seen its population grow to over 1,000 thanks to sustained conservation efforts. These success stories show that extinction is not inevitable — but they also show that significant effort, funding, and commitment from governments, organisations, and local communities are essential.

Passage 5: Insects — The World's Most Important Animals
Topic: Biology & Ecology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Insects are by far the most numerous and diverse group of animals on Earth. There are over one million known species of insects — more than all other animal species combined — and scientists believe millions more have not yet been discovered. Insects live in almost every habitat on Earth, from tropical rainforests and deserts to freshwater streams and high mountain slopes. They range enormously in size, from tiny wasps less than a millimetre long to the Goliath beetle, which can be as large as a human hand. Despite often being overlooked or considered a nuisance, insects play essential roles in the world's ecosystems that make them arguably the most important group of animals on the planet.
Paragraph B
One of the most important roles that insects play is pollination. Many plants, including a large proportion of the food crops that humans depend on, cannot reproduce without the help of insects to transfer pollen from one flower to another. Bees are the best-known pollinators — both wild bees and the domestic honeybee are essential for the production of fruits, vegetables, and nuts. But many other insects also pollinate flowers, including butterflies, moths, beetles, and flies. It is estimated that around a third of all the food humans eat depends on pollination by insects. Without insects, many plant species would be unable to reproduce, food supplies would fall dramatically, and many other animals would lose their food sources.
Paragraph C
Insects also play other vital roles in ecosystems. Decomposer insects — such as beetles, ants, and flies — break down dead plant and animal material, returning nutrients to the soil and helping it stay fertile. Dung beetles perform a particularly important service, burying animal dung, which improves soil quality and reduces the spread of diseases. Insects are also a critical food source for a huge number of other animals — including birds, bats, fish, reptiles, and small mammals. Many species of birds feed their chicks almost entirely on insects. Some insects are natural predators of pest species — ladybirds, for example, eat aphids that damage crops, reducing the need for chemical pesticides.
Paragraph D
Despite their importance, insect populations are declining rapidly in many parts of the world. Studies suggest that the total mass of insects on Earth has fallen by as much as 75% in some areas over the past few decades. The main causes are habitat loss — particularly the loss of wildflower meadows and hedgerows — the widespread use of pesticides in farming, light pollution that disrupts nocturnal insects, and climate change. The decline of insects is very worrying because of the enormous number of other species that depend on them. Many bird populations that feed on insects have also declined significantly. Protecting insect populations requires reducing pesticide use, protecting and restoring natural habitats such as wildflower meadows, and reducing light pollution in rural areas.

Passage 6: Social Animals — Life in Groups
Topic: Animal Behaviour  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Many animals live and work together in groups rather than alone. Living in a group offers important advantages for survival. Animals in groups can work together to find food, defend territory, raise young, and protect each other from predators. A single meerkat watching out for eagles and hawks while the rest of the group feeds is a classic example — one animal takes the risk of standing guard while others eat safely. Being part of a group also allows animals to hunt prey that they could not catch alone. A wolf pack, for example, can bring down a large elk that no single wolf could overpower. However, living in groups also has disadvantages — animals must share resources and food, and disease can spread more easily in a group.
Paragraph B
Some of the most impressive examples of social behaviour in the animal kingdom can be found among insects. Honeybees live in colonies of up to 60,000 individuals, all working together with remarkable efficiency. Every bee has a specific role. The queen bee's only job is to lay eggs. Worker bees — all female — collect pollen and nectar, make honey, feed young bees, guard the hive, and build and repair the honeycomb. Drone bees — all male — exist only to mate with a new queen. Ants and termites also live in highly organised colonies of millions of individuals, with different individuals specialised for different tasks. These "superorganisms" — where the colony functions almost like a single living thing — are among the most successful life forms on Earth.
Paragraph C
Among mammals, primates — monkeys and apes — show some of the most complex social behaviours. Chimpanzees live in communities of up to 150 individuals and have a complex social hierarchy — a ranking system in which some individuals have more status and power than others. Higher-ranking chimps have better access to food and mates. Chimps also form friendships, cooperate in hunting, share food, and have been observed using simple tools — such as sticks to extract termites from their mounds. Dolphins live in social groups called pods, communicate with each other through a range of sounds and body movements, and cooperate in fishing — surrounding schools of fish together to make them easier to catch.
Paragraph D
Social behaviour in animals has long fascinated scientists because it raises interesting questions about the origins of cooperation, communication, and even culture. Some animals appear to pass on behaviours to younger generations through learning — rather than instinct alone. Chimpanzees in different parts of Africa use different tools in different ways, suggesting that local "cultures" exist among them. Killer whales (orcas) in different parts of the world have different hunting strategies, vocalisations, and social customs that are passed down through family groups. Research into animal social behaviour has not only deepened our understanding of other species but has also shed light on the evolution of human social behaviour, language, and culture.

Passage 7: Oceans — The Blue Heart of Our Planet
Topic: Marine Biology & Ecosystems  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The world's oceans cover more than 70% of the Earth's surface and are the largest living space on the planet. They are home to an extraordinary variety of life — from microscopic plankton that float near the surface to giant blue whales, the largest animals that have ever lived. Scientists estimate that the oceans contain around 250,000 known marine species, but they believe the real number could be much higher — perhaps over a million — as large parts of the deep ocean remain unexplored. The ocean is not just important for the wildlife that lives in it. It plays a crucial role in regulating the Earth's climate, providing food for billions of people, and absorbing a large proportion of the carbon dioxide that humans produce.
Paragraph B
Life in the ocean exists at every depth. The sunlit zone — the top 200 metres of the ocean — is where most sea life is found, because sunlight can reach here and allow photosynthesis to occur. Phytoplankton — tiny plant-like organisms — float here in enormous numbers and are the foundation of the ocean food chain. Below 200 metres, light fades and conditions become harder. Yet life still exists in the deep ocean, where temperatures are near freezing, the pressure is enormous, and there is no sunlight at all. Deep-sea creatures have evolved remarkable adaptations to survive here — including transparent bodies, enormous eyes to detect the faintest light, and the ability to produce their own light through a process called bioluminescence.
Paragraph C
Coral reefs are one of the most important and biodiverse marine ecosystems. They are built by tiny animals called coral polyps, which secrete hard calcium carbonate skeletons over thousands of years. Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor, but they support an estimated 25% of all marine species. They provide food and shelter for fish, turtles, and countless other creatures, and they also protect coastlines from wave damage. Unfortunately, coral reefs are seriously threatened. Warming ocean water — caused by climate change — causes corals to expel the algae that live inside them and give them their colour, turning them white. This is called coral bleaching. If temperatures remain high for too long, the corals die. Pollution and human activity such as destructive fishing practices also damage reefs.
Paragraph D
Human activity threatens ocean health in many ways. Overfishing — catching fish faster than they can reproduce — has dramatically reduced populations of many fish species. Around 34% of the world's fish stocks are currently fished at unsustainable levels. Plastic pollution is a serious problem — millions of tonnes of plastic enter the ocean every year and do not break down, harming marine life that swallows or becomes tangled in it. Chemical pollution from farming, factories, and cities contaminates coastal waters. Climate change is warming and acidifying the ocean, threatening species that are unable to adapt. Governments and international organisations are working to establish marine protected areas — zones where fishing and other damaging activities are restricted — and to reduce plastic waste and greenhouse gas emissions to protect the ocean for future generations.

Passage 8: Animal Intelligence — How Smart Are Animals?
Topic: Animal Behaviour & Cognition  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
For a long time, many scientists believed that intelligence — the ability to think, learn, and solve problems — was something that only humans truly possessed. Animals were thought to act mainly on instinct — automatic, inborn responses to situations — rather than through conscious thought. However, research over the past few decades has shown that many animals are far more intelligent than previously thought. They can learn from experience, solve problems, use tools, communicate in complex ways, and even show signs of emotions such as happiness, sadness, and empathy. This growing understanding of animal intelligence has changed the way many scientists and ordinary people think about the relationship between humans and other animals.
Paragraph B
Some of the most impressive examples of animal intelligence come from great apes — chimpanzees, gorillas, bonobos, and orangutans. These animals share a large proportion of their DNA with humans and show many human-like behaviours. Chimpanzees have been taught to communicate using sign language and have demonstrated the ability to understand and use hundreds of signs. They can solve complex puzzles, recognise themselves in mirrors — which suggests self-awareness — and use a variety of tools. Orangutans have been observed making simple shelters from leaves and branches. Gorillas in captivity have been known to use sticks to test the depth of water before wading through it. These abilities suggest that problem-solving and planning ahead are not uniquely human skills.
Paragraph C
Intelligence is not limited to mammals. Octopuses are among the most intelligent invertebrates — animals without a backbone — known to science. They can open jars to access food inside, navigate complex mazes, solve puzzles, and even seem to play. They have relatively large brains for their body size and have been observed using coconut shells as portable shelters, which is a form of tool use. Crows and ravens — a family of birds — are also remarkably intelligent. They can solve multi-step problems, use tools, remember human faces, and even appear to plan for the future. In one famous experiment, New Caledonian crows used a short stick to reach a longer stick, which they then used to reach food — demonstrating the ability to use tools to get other tools.
Paragraph D
Scientists use several ways to measure animal intelligence. One common test is the "mirror test" — placing an animal in front of a mirror and seeing if it recognises the reflection as itself. Only a small number of animals — including great apes, elephants, dolphins, and magpies — have passed this test, suggesting they have some level of self-awareness. Another measure is the ability to use tools. Another is the ability to understand cause and effect — knowing that one action will lead to another. Scientists are careful, however, not to assume that intelligence in animals works in exactly the same way as in humans. Different species have evolved different types of intelligence suited to their own environments and ways of life, and comparing animal and human intelligence directly is not always straightforward.

Passage 9: Forests — The Lungs of the Earth
Topic: Ecosystems & Biology  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Forests are one of the most important ecosystems on Earth. They cover about 31% of the land surface of the planet and are home to around 80% of all land-based animal and plant species. Forests are sometimes called the "lungs of the Earth" because they absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and release oxygen through the process of photosynthesis. They also play a critical role in regulating the water cycle — trees absorb water from the soil through their roots and release it into the air through their leaves, helping to maintain rainfall patterns. Forests provide shelter, food, and habitat for an enormous number of species, and they also provide many resources and services that human societies depend on.
Paragraph B
There are several main types of forest. Tropical rainforests are found near the equator, where temperatures are warm and rainfall is very high throughout the year. These forests are the most biodiverse on Earth — a single hectare may contain more species of trees than the whole of Europe. The Amazon rainforest in South America is the largest tropical rainforest in the world, covering around 5.5 million square kilometres and containing around 10% of all species on Earth. Temperate forests are found in cooler regions of Europe, North America, and parts of Asia. They have four distinct seasons and are home to familiar trees like oak, beech, and maple. Boreal forests — also called "taiga" — stretch across northern Canada, Russia, and Scandinavia and are the largest land ecosystem on Earth, covering vast areas with conifer trees like pine and spruce.
Paragraph C
Forests provide a wide range of benefits to both wildlife and human communities. They are a major source of food — fruits, nuts, berries, and mushrooms are eaten directly, and many medicines are derived from forest plants. Timber — wood from trees — is used for building, furniture, paper, and fuel. Forests also protect communities from natural hazards. Tree roots hold the soil together, preventing erosion. On slopes, forests reduce the risk of landslides. Along rivers, forests absorb excess water and reduce the risk of flooding. Many local and indigenous communities around the world have lived in and depended on forests for thousands of years, and forests have great cultural and spiritual significance for these communities.
Paragraph D
Despite their importance, the world's forests are disappearing at an alarming rate. It is estimated that around 4.7 million hectares of forest — an area roughly the size of Switzerland — are lost every year. The main causes are farming, logging, and urban development. In tropical countries, large areas of rainforest are cleared to create farmland for crops such as soy and oil palm, or for cattle ranching. This loss has serious consequences — it destroys the habitat of countless species, releases large amounts of stored carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, and disrupts the water cycle. Many governments, organisations, and communities are working to protect remaining forests and to restore areas that have been destroyed, including through replanting and by paying local communities to protect the forests in their area.

Passage 10: Animal Adaptations — Surviving in Extreme Environments
Topic: Biology & Evolution  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Life exists in almost every environment on Earth — from the frozen polar ice caps to scorching hot deserts, from the deepest parts of the ocean to the highest mountain peaks. In each of these environments, the animals that live there have developed special features and behaviours that help them survive in conditions that would be deadly for most other species. These special features are called "adaptations." Adaptations develop over many generations through the process of natural selection — the idea, first described by Charles Darwin, that individuals with features that help them survive and reproduce in their environment are more likely to pass those features on to their offspring.
Paragraph B
Animals that live in cold environments have developed a range of adaptations to help them survive the freezing temperatures. Polar bears have very thick fur that traps warm air close to the body, and a thick layer of fat called blubber under the skin that provides insulation. Their paws are large and covered with tiny bumps that give them grip on ice. Arctic foxes change the colour of their fur — brown in summer, white in winter — allowing them to camouflage themselves in the snow and sneak up on prey or avoid predators. Penguins huddle together in large groups to share body heat, and their feathers are waterproof, keeping them dry and warm in icy water. Emperor penguins can dive deeper and hold their breath longer than almost any other bird, allowing them to catch fish under ice.
Paragraph C
Desert animals face the opposite challenge — extreme heat, intense sunlight, and very little water. Camels are perhaps the most famous desert animal, and their adaptations are well known, though some are often misunderstood. Camels do not store water in their humps — they store fat, which they can convert to energy when food is scarce. They are, however, able to drink enormous amounts of water very quickly — up to 130 litres at one time — and their bodies are very efficient at conserving water. Fennec foxes have enormous ears that help release heat from the body, keeping them cool. Many desert animals are nocturnal — they sleep in the shade or underground during the hot day and are only active at night when it is cooler.
Paragraph D
Some of the most extreme adaptations are found in animals that live in the deep ocean, where there is no sunlight, temperatures are near freezing, and the pressure is enormous. Anglerfish have a long bioluminescent lure that hangs in front of their face and glows in the dark, attracting prey towards their mouth. Some deep-sea creatures have transparent bodies, making them very difficult to see in the dark water. The tardigrade — a tiny animal less than a millimetre long — holds the record for the most extreme survival ability of any animal on Earth. Tardigrades can survive extreme heat, extreme cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of outer space. They do this by going into a state of suspended animation — almost completely stopping their body processes — until conditions improve.

questions.prompt: What is the primary method of communication used by most animals?
questions.explanation: According to the passage, most animals cannot use spoken language to communicate and instead rely on sounds, body language, scent, colour, and movement to get their messages across.

options.label: Sounds, body language, scent, colour, and movement

questions.prompt: Is it true that the Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any animal on Earth?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any animal on Earth, travelling from its breeding grounds in the Arctic all the way to Antarctica and back every year.

questions.prompt: What is the main reason why species become endangered?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the most common reason why species become endangered is habitat destruction, which includes deforestation, urban development, and the draining of wetlands.
The passage states that insects play a crucial role in pollination, with many plants relying on them to transfer pollen from one flower to another.

questions.prompt: What is the term used to describe the process by which animals break down dead plant and animal material and return nutrients to the soil?
questions.explanation: The passage states that decomposer insects, such as beetles, ants, and flies, break down dead plant and animal material and return nutrients to the soil.

questions.prompt: Is it true that coral reefs are found in the deep ocean?
questions.explanation: The passage states that coral reefs are found in shallow waters, typically in the sunlit zone, and are built by tiny animals called coral polyps.

questions.prompt: What is the term used to describe the ability of an animal to recognize itself in a mirror?
questions.explanation: The passage states that self-awareness is demonstrated by animals that can recognize themselves in a mirror, such as great apes, elephants, dolphins, and magpies.

questions.prompt: Are forests important for regulating the water cycle?
questions.explanation: The passage states that forests play a critical role in regulating the water cycle, with trees absorbing water from the soil through their roots and releasing it into the air through their leaves.

questions.prompt: What is the term used to describe the special features and behaviors that help animals survive in extreme environments?
questions.explanation: The passage states that adaptations are special features and behaviors that help animals survive in extreme environments, such as the thick fur of polar bears or the ability of camels to conserve water.

questions.prompt: Is it true that the tardigrade can survive in extreme conditions such as extreme heat, cold, and radiation?
questions.explanation: The passage states that the tardigrade holds the record for the most extreme survival ability of any animal on Earth, able to survive extreme heat, cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of outer space.
